The devaraja cult was clearly a royal cult designed to legitimate and maximize the king’s power by endowing divinity or quasi-divinity onto kingship. The Indian ritual was the most sophisticated and hence most powerful ideological device available at the time  ...    what the cult was to bring down to this world was legitimacy and authority of rulers over the ruled. Sublimity and godliness emanated by gigantic shrines and conspicuously placed lingas could effectively produce a religious basis for the legitimation of the kingship (Kulke 1978; Harris 2005, 19). title: How did the religious beliefs of Khmer kings influence Angkor Wat's tr
highlights:
beliefs of Khmer kings deeply influenced Angkor Wat’s transformation from a Hindu temple to a Buddhist  ...    Angkor Wat Bronze

## 1. Divine Kingship and Hindu Foundations under Early Khmer Kings The concept of divine kingship (devarāja), established by King Jayavarman II in 802 AD, declared the king as a god-king and a cosmic ruler embodying the Hindu god Shiva. This idea placed the monarch at the center of the universe’s order, legitimizing royal authority through sacred symbolism like the royal linga (a phallic symbol representing Shiva).  ...    The religious beliefs of Khmer kings were intertwined with their political authority. They saw themselves as upholders of cosmic order and mediators between heaven and earth. The elaborate temple architecture, rich symbolism, and devotional art served to both manifest and reinforce royal legitimacy. Kings were divine or semi-divine figures whose reigns were sanctioned by Hindu or Buddhist cosmology,  ...    Early Khmer kings, especially
